Your financial data tells a clear story — you have strong income coming in, but there are a few recurring charges worth a second look. Here are three savings opportunities you can act on this week, each backed by a specific transaction in your history.
Opportunity 1: Reconsider your Adobe subscription
Your transactions show a recurring $59.99/month charge to Adobe (most recently May 14). That's $719.88 per year. The question worth asking this week: are you actively using Adobe's creative suite, or is this a habit holdover?
If you use it professionally for freelance work (you did earn $1,200 from Figma consulting in May), it may be worth keeping. But if you're only using one tool occasionally — say, just Acrobat for PDFs — Adobe offers single-app plans starting around $20-25/month, which could save you roughly $35/month or $420/year. If you're barely using it, canceling entirely saves the full $59.99/month.
To verify this week: open Adobe Creative Cloud and check your last-used dates across each app.
Opportunity 2: Audit your transportation overlap (Uber + Lyft)
Your transactions show both Uber ($34.10 on May 22) and a recurring Lyft subscription ($27.80/month, flagged as a monthly recurring charge). Paying a monthly Lyft subscription and still using Uber on top of it is a classic double-spend pattern.
If you're paying Lyft's monthly membership to get discounts on rides but still booking Uber when it's cheaper or more convenient, the membership fee may not be delivering full value. The Lyft recurring charge alone runs $333.60/year. Canceling it — or consolidating to whichever app you actually prefer — could recover most of that.
To verify this week: check how many Lyft rides you took in May versus the $27.80 membership cost to see if the discount earned exceeds the fee.
Opportunity 3: Shop your AT&T Wireless plan
Your phone bill shows $72/month to AT&T Wireless (May 9, recurring). For a single-line plan, that's $864/year — on the higher end for today's market. Comparable single-line plans from carriers like Mint Mobile, Visible, or even AT&T's own prepaid tier run $25–45/month.
Switching to a $35/month plan would save roughly $37/month or $444/year, with no change in your day-to-day phone experience.
To verify this week: log into your AT&T account, check your current data usage vs. your plan's allowance, and compare against current prepaid or MVNO offers.
Combined potential impact: Canceling Adobe entirely + dropping the Lyft membership + switching phone carriers could free up roughly $160–$170/month ($1,900–$2,000/year) — and none of these require cutting something you'd truly miss.
Would you like help thinking through which of these would have the least impact on your daily routine?
